era_context: The late 13th century saw the Yuan Dynasty at its height under Kublai Khan, the Mamluk Sultanate dominant in the Eastern Mediterranean and Levant, and European feudal kingdoms in complex competition. In Southeast Asia, Thai kingdoms were rising while Khmer power declined. Islamic scholarship and trade networks remained globally significant. This was a period of relative stability in many regions following decades of conquest and warfare.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe (beyond major kingdoms)

events.title: Mongol Invasion of Vietnam (Tran Dynasty Victory)
events.description: The Tran Dynasty of Vietnam, under Emperor Tran Nhan Tong, defeated a major Mongol invasion force sent by Kublai Khan. The Vietnamese forces, commanded by General Tran Hung Dao, employed guerrilla tactics and naval warfare to repel the Mongol army. This victory was significant because it demonstrated that Mongol military dominance was not absolute and that smaller, well-organized states could resist Mongol conquest. The Tran Dynasty's success established Vietnam as an independent power and prevented Mongol conquest of Southeast Asia.

sources.name: Dai Viet Su Ky Toan Thu (Complete History of Great Viet, compiled 15th century but based on earlier sources)

events.certainty_note: The Mongol invasion of Vietnam in 1287 is well-attested in both Vietnamese and Chinese sources. The date is secure, though specific details of the battle vary between sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Mongol expansion attempts in Southeast Asia
Precedes later Mongol invasions of Vietnam (1285-1287 campaign)

events.title: Qalawun Consolidates Mamluk Power in Egypt and Levant
events.description: Sultan Qalawun of the Mamluk Sultanate continued to consolidate power in Egypt and the Levant during 1287. He strengthened the Mamluk military apparatus, reformed the administration, and prepared for the final assault on Acre (which would occur in 1289). Qalawun's reign (1279-1290) was marked by military victories, administrative reforms, and the expansion of Mamluk power in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa bi-Akhbar al-A'imma al-Khulafa (Egyptian chronicle, written early 15th century)
Ibn al-Athir and other Islamic historians (later compilations)

events.certainty_note: Qalawun's consolidation of power was a gradual process throughout the 1280s. Specific attribution to 1287 is approximate; the broader process is well-attested.

events.title: Kublai Khan Continues Yuan Administrative Reforms
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under Kublai Khan continued administrative consolidation and integration of Han Chinese bureaucratic traditions. The 1280s saw increasing Han Chinese influence in Yuan governance, with examination systems and Confucian scholarship gaining prominence. These reforms aimed to create a hybrid Mongol-Han Chinese administrative system.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official Yuan Dynasty history, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Administrative reforms were ongoing throughout the 1280s. Specific attribution to 1287 is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The 1287 Mongol invasion of Vietnam is sometimes conflated with earlier invasions (1258, 1285-1287). Modern scholarship clarifies that there were multiple Mongol invasion attempts, with the major campaigns occurring in 1285-1287, culminating in Vietnamese victory. The exact date of the decisive battle (sometimes called the Battle of Bach Dang River) is debated, with some sources placing it in 1287 and others in 1288. Additionally, the traditional narrative emphasizes Tran Hung Dao's genius, but modern scholars note that Vietnamese success resulted from multiple factors including geography, logistics, and Mongol overextension.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1287 is moderate but regionally uneven. Vietnamese sources are later compilations subject to nationalist embellishment. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) are official court histories compiled centuries later, subject to dynastic bias. Islamic sources for Mamluk activities are similarly retrospective. European sources are largely absent for this year. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, Northern Europe, and the Pacific are underrepresented or absent from surviving documentation. The historical record privileges military and political events over economic, social, or cultural developments.

graph_edges.note: Part of broader Mongol invasion campaign in Vietnam (1285-1287)
Vietnamese victory established foundation for later independence movements
Qalawun's consolidation enabled final assault on Acre
